Of the Oracle found inscribed an a Stone, when the Walls of Chalcedon were demolished by Order of the Emperor Valens.

ccel.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

source.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

title: Of the Oracle found inscribed an a Stone, when the Walls of Chalcedon were demolished by Order of the Emperor Valens.

source.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: An order was issued by the emperor that the walls of Chalcedon, a city opposite to Byzantium, should be demolished: for he had sworn to do this, after he should have conquered the usurper, because the Chalcedonians had sided with the usurper, and had used insulting language toward Valens, and shut their gates against him as he passed by their city.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: In consequence of the imperial decree, therefore, the walls were razed and the stones were conveyed to Constantinople to serve for the formation of the public baths which are called Constantianæ.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: On one of these stones an oracle was found engraven, which had lain concealed for a long time, in which it was predicted that when the city should be supplied with abundance of water, then should the wall serve for a bath; and that innumerable hordes of barbarous nations having overrun the provinces of the Roman empire, and done a great deal of mischief, should themselves at length be destroyed.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: We shall here insert this oracle for the gratification of the studious:

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: ‘When nymphs their mystic dance with wat’ry feet

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Shall tread through proud Byzantium’s stately street;

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: When rage the city wall shall overthrow,

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Whose stones to fence a bathing-place shall go:

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Then savage lands shall send forth myriad swarms,

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Adorned with golden locks aud burnished arms,

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: That having Ister’s silver streams o’erpast,

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Shall Scythian fields and Mœsia’s meadows waste.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: But when with conquest flushed they enter Thrace,

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Fate shall assign them there a burial-place.’

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Such was the prophecy.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: And indeed it afterwards happened, that when Valens by building an aqueduct supplied Constantinople with abundance of water, the barbarous nations made various irruptions, as we shall hereafter see.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: But it happened that some explained the prediction otherwise.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: For when that aqueduct was completed, Clearchus the prefect of the city built a stately bath, to which the name of ‘the Plentiful Water’ was given, in that which is now called the Forum of Theodosius: on which account the people celebrated a festival with great rejoicings, whereby there was, say they, an accomplishment of those words of the oracle,

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: ‘their mystic dance with wat’ry feet

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Shall tread through proud Byzantium’s stately street.’

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: But the completion of the prophecy took place afterwards.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: While the demolition was in progress the Constantinopolitans besought the emperor to suspend the destruction of the walls; and the inhabitants of Nicomedia and Nicæa sending from Bithynia to Constantinople, made the same request.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: But the emperor being exceedingly exasperated against the Chalcedonians, was with difficulty prevailed upon to listen to these petitions in their favor: but that he might perform his oath, he commanded that the walls should be pulled down, while at the same time the breaches should be repaired by being filled up with other small stones.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Whence it is that in the present day one may see in certain parts of the wall very inferior materials laid upon prodigiously large stones, forming those unsightly patches which were made on that occasion.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: So much will be sufficient on the walls of Chalcedon.
